parsed.message:
The internal steel cylinder of your geyser has corroded through, allowing water to escape into the safety drip tray. When the protective sacrificial anode inside the tank is fully consumed, the water begins to attack the steel shell, leading to rust and eventual structural failure. This explains the dark brown, rust-stained water pooling in your drip tray and the drop in hot water temperature as cold water constantly dilutes the cylinder.

This is a progressive failure that cannot be repaired by replacing individual valves or fittings. The entire geyser unit will need to be replaced by a registered plumber to prevent the tank from rupturing completely and causing extensive water damage to your ceilings and floor boards.
parsed.action_required: Your technician will isolate the electrical supply and water mains, drain the remaining water from the failed cylinder, and remove the corroded unit. They will then install a new high-pressure geyser, replace the safety valves, and ensure the drip tray and overflow pipes are correctly aligned. Finally, they will commission the system and provide a compliance certificate.
image_descriptions: A close-up of the geyser cylinder showing the manufacturer label, a brass T-junction fitting, and a black plastic safety valve dripping directly into a tray filled with dark brown, rusty water.
An under-sink or wall-cavity view showing copper and white plastic pipes passing through a partition wall with dark water stains spreading across the timber shelf below.
An exterior view of a multi-unit residential corridor showing the external wall and window frame where the geyser overflow pipes exit the building.

contractor_checklist: Isolate the electrical supply at the main distribution board and disconnect the element.
Drain the corroded geyser cylinder completely into the waste system.
Remove the failed geyser and inspect the existing drip tray for structural integrity.
Install a new geyser unit and connect it to the existing hot and cold water reticulation.
Replace the temperature and pressure safety valves to comply with local building regulations.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Switch off the geyser circuit breaker at your main distribution board and close the cold-water stopcock leading to the geyser to stop the leak.
parsed.diy_verification: Check the external overflow pipe on the outside wall; if it is dry but the drip tray inside is filling with rusty water, the leak is coming directly from the tank shell.

User reports increased electricity bills and loss of hot water capacity
Staining on the support structure confirms an active, ongoing leak

parsed.message:
The dark, rust-coloured water pooling in your drip tray indicates that the geyser's internal cylinder has corroded. When the protective sacrificial anode inside the tank is fully consumed, the water begins to attack the steel tank itself, leading to rust and eventual structural failure. The active dripping around the valve fitting is a direct symptom of this internal pressure and shell degradation.

This is a progressive failure that will continue to deteriorate. If left unresolved, the weakened tank shell can rupture completely, leading to a sudden flood that will overwhelm the drip tray and cause extensive water damage to your ceilings and floor boards below.
parsed.action_required: Your technician will isolate the water and electrical supply to the geyser. They will then drain the remaining water from the system, remove the corroded cylinder, and install a new geyser compliant with local plumbing standards.

parsed.message:
Your garage door is failing to open because the tension spring on the left-hand side is completely missing. These heavy wooden tilt-up doors rely on a matched pair of tension springs to counterbalance their weight, allowing the motor to lift the door with minimal effort. Without the left spring, the door is severely unbalanced and too heavy for the motor to lift safely.

When you attempt to operate the door, the motor detects this extreme resistance as an obstruction. To prevent damage to its internal gears or the door itself, the motor's safety system automatically stops the operation, beeps to signal an overload, and reverses the door back to its closed position.
parsed.action_required: Your technician will inspect the remaining spring to determine the correct weight rating and length required for your door. They will then install a matching pair of new tension springs to ensure even balance and tension across both sides. Finally, the specialist will test the door's manual balance and recalibrate the motor's limit and force settings.
image_descriptions: A close-up view of the left-hand side hinge mechanism showing the black metal bracket mounted to the wall and door, with no tension spring attached.
A wide view of the entire wooden tilt-up garage door from inside, showing a tension spring on the right side but a completely empty space where the spring should be on the left side.
A close-up of the ceiling-mounted motor rail and the red emergency release cord hanging down near the top edge of the wooden door.
A detailed view of the metal towing bracket bolted to the top centre of the wooden garage door panel, showing the connecting arm secured with a clevis pin.

A detailed view of the metal towing bracket bolted to the top centre of the wooden garage door panel, showing the connecting arm secured with a clevis pin.
clarification_questions: No, the left spring is missing.

answer_chips.text: No, the left spring is missing.

Adjust the spring tension until the door remains balanced and stable when operated manually.
Recalibrate the garage door opener force limits and travel limits to ensure smooth automated operation.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Disconnect the garage door opener from the power outlet to prevent anyone from accidentally operating the motor while the door is unbalanced.
parsed.diy_verification: With the motor disengaged via the red release cord, try to lift the door halfway by hand; if it is extremely heavy and falls shut immediately, the counterbalance system has failed.

parsed.message:
Your garage door is hanging crookedly because the left-side tension spring is completely missing. On a tilt-up garage door, these heavy springs on either side act as a counterbalance to offset the immense weight of the wooden panel, allowing the motor to lift it smoothly. Without the left spring, the entire weight of that side drags down, jamming the door in its frame and putting severe twisting strain on the central motor arm.

Tension springs fatigue over thousands of open and close cycles, eventually snapping under high tension or slipping off their mounting hooks. Operating the door in this unbalanced state can quickly burn out your opener motor or bend the steel lifting arms. Because these springs are under extreme mechanical tension, they are highly dangerous to handle and must only be replaced and calibrated by a trained technician.
parsed.action_required: Your technician will safely relieve any remaining tension in the system, replace the missing left-side tension spring, and inspect the right-side spring for signs of metal fatigue. They will then re-hang the door, balance the tension on both sides so the door sits perfectly level, and test the opener motor to ensure it operates without straining.

A full view of the wooden tilt-up door hanging crookedly, showing a long tension spring on the right side and an empty space on the left side.
A view looking up at the ceiling-mounted motor rail and the red emergency release cord hanging down near the top of the wooden door.
A close-up of the silver metal towing bracket bolted to the top centre of the wooden door panel, showing the connecting arm pinned in place.

A close-up of the silver metal towing bracket bolted to the top centre of the wooden door panel, showing the connecting arm pinned in place.
clarification_questions: No, but the bracket is empty.

answer_chips.text: No, but the bracket is empty.

Install a new tension spring on the left-side mechanism matched to the door weight.
Align and level the wooden door panel within the frame opening.
Adjust and balance the tension on both sides to ensure smooth manual and automated operation.
parsed.homeowner_prep: Disconnect the garage door opener from the power outlet and avoid attempting to open or close the door manually.
parsed.diy_verification: With the motor disengaged via the red cord, try to lift the door slightly; if the left side feels extremely heavy and refuses to rise evenly, the left counterbalance spring is broken or missing.

The crooked angle of the door matches a single-sided spring failure
